modelDescription: An OC3-level threat actor (a small cybercrime syndicate of approximately ten individuals with resources of around $1M) operates as a ransomware affiliate using RaaS services. The group conducts double extortion attacks — both encrypting data and exfiltrating it — against SMEs with revenue of $10M–$50M. Initial access is achieved by exploiting vulnerabilities in public-facing web applications or devices, targeting organisations at cybersecurity maturity level MIL1 with MIL2 in some domains.

scenarioInputs.heading: Type of Actor
scenarioInputs.content:
* We consider a threat actor that would broadly be classified as a small cybercrime syndicate having OC3 level operational capacity (~10 individuals, with resources of ~$1m, and loosely based on the corresponding threat actor definition provided in [RAND](https://www.rand.org/pubs/research_reports/RRA2849-1.html)).
* The threat actor is assumed to operate primarily as a RaaS affiliate (per definitions in [NCSC](https://www.ncsc.gov.uk/whitepaper/ransomware-extortion-and-the-cyber-crime-ecosystem)), and specialises in achieving initial access by exploiting vulnerabilities in public-facing web apps and devices.
* The threat actor is expected to bring their own customisations to the task of deploying the RaaS operator's capabilities, and has the capability to adaptively make use of tools, and 'living off the land' approaches in moving through the attacked network (per [CISA provided information on LockBit affiliates](https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories/aa23-165a)).
* The threat actor is assumed to have pre-existing cyberattack infrastructure, but no pre-existing access to the attacked organisation.
* In our scenario, we envisage some division of labour, with different members of the cybercrime syndicate optionally specialising in different areas, such as malware customisation, infrastructure operation/acquisition, network penetration, negotiation/coercion, crypto-laundering etc. It should be noted that the RaaS operator may perform many of these activities on behalf of the affiliate, and also that it is commonplace for the affiliate to just provide the initial access, conduct the attack, exfiltrate the data and leave everything else including malware development, negotiation and payment, crypto-laundering etc to the RaaS operator. Appendix B - contains more info on ransomware crime syndicate group size.

scenarioInputs.heading: Type of Target
scenarioInputs.content:
* The threat actor targets small, medium enterprises (SME), with bias toward the more 'medium' sized companies, and hoping to realise a median ransom payout of somewhere on the order of $100k
* Approximate company sizes of interest in our scenario are:
  * 75-250 employees
  * Revenue $10m-$50m
  * Note: The size of an 'SME' as defined by the EU, and as described in [wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Small_and_medium_enterprises) is 50-250 employees and $2m-$50m in revenue. So based on this definition, the companies we consider in our scenario are more biased toward the 'medium' end of the SME bracket.
* Ideal targets are sufficiently financially attractive, that turnover/resources are sufficient to lead to the targeted ~$100k median ransom payout.
  * Note, according to [Sophos](https://www.sophos.com/en-us/content/state-of-ransomware) (edit: now accessible [here](https://github.com/jacobdjwilson/awesome-annual-security-reports/blob/main/Annual%20Security%20Reports/2024/Sophos-State-of-Ransomware-2024.pdf)) (2024), the median payout for a $10m-$50m company in 2024 was $330k. Whilst according to [Sophos (2025)](https://www.sophos.com/en-us/content/state-of-ransomware) the median demand was $109k.
  * Whilst the end ransom demand for a smaller company may be less than for a larger one, compromise of a smaller and relatively less well defended company may be easier, and less prone to result in the attention of law enforcement.
* The threat actor especially targets companies relying on IT systems for operations, e.g. logistics, manufacturing, retail, such that encryption ransomware, and its associated loss of availability for the target has significant impact (in addition to the impact of confidentiality loss associated with data exfiltration).

scenarioInputs.heading: Type of Vector
scenarioInputs.content:
* From [Sophos](https://www.sophos.com/en-us/content/state-of-ransomware) (2024) (edit: now accessible [here](https://github.com/jacobdjwilson/awesome-annual-security-reports/blob/main/Annual%20Security%20Reports/2024/Sophos-State-of-Ransomware-2024.pdf)) (also reproduced in Appendix D), it can be seen that ransomware affiliates may use a variety of initial access vectors.
  * Exploited vulnerability was the top 'root cause' for companies with revenue < $10m (at 28%), and was the second most common root cause for companies with revenue $10m-$50m at 25%.
* Whilst [Sophos (2025)](https://www.sophos.com/en-us/content/state-of-ransomware) indicates that for companies with between 100 and 250 employees 'exploited vulnerability' was the root cause in 29% of incidents, coming second only to 'compromised credentials' on 30%)
* Note that info from [Coveware](https://www.coveware.com/blog/2024/1/25/new-ransomware-reporting-requirements-kick-in-as-victims-increasingly-avoid-paying?utm_source=chatgpt.com) (reproduced in Appendix D) suggests that CVEs are also often favoured by actors going after bigger enterprise targets
* [NCSC](https://www.ncsc.gov.uk/whitepaper/ransomware-extortion-and-the-cyber-crime-ecosystem) also identified 'direct exploitation' as a prominent initial access vector for ransomware
  * ".... many [criminals]…conduct the scanning themselves…. Criminals look for devices that are likely to be in businesses (rather than home environments). Examples include Microsoft Exchange servers, platforms such as Citrix or VMware, VPN devices and firewall devices".
  * "Criminal use of exploits often surges shortly after certain critical patches are released indicating they are being reverse engineered from the patches. In most cases, an exploit is widely available in the criminal forums in less than one week from the patch being released. A zero-day exploit is a recently discovered vulnerability, not yet known to vendors or antivirus companies, that criminals can exploit. Cyber criminals don't need to develop their own zero-day exploits as doing so is expensive, and there are many devices 'in the wild' that are not patched regularly. However, some actors have been known to use zero-day exploits, most notably there are public reports of Cl0p's use of the Accellion, GoAnywhere and MOVEit vulnerabilities".
* Example type of vector
  * According to [RecordedFuture](https://www.recordedfuture.com/research/patterns-targets-ransomware-exploitation-vulnerabilities-2017-2023), certain classes of threat actors most favour vulnerabilities that have high impact in terms of access or control over systems and the ubiquity of the affected software. They also state "... we also found that when many ransomware groups target a vulnerability, they do so almost always because it can be exploited with minimal lines of malicious code that can be easily implemented into mass scanning activity (through malicious HTTP requests, for example)"
  * Some example vulnerabilities that that LockBit affiliates have used, according to [CISA](https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories/aa23-165a) include:
    * [CVE-2019-0708](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2019-0708): Microsoft Remote Desktop Services Remote Code Execution Vulnerability
    * [CVE-2018-13379](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2018-13379): Fortinet FortiOS Secure Sockets Layer (SSL) Virtual Private Network (VPN) Path Traversal Vulnerability.
  * Some examples that Ghost used [(SC media)](https://www.scworld.com/news/fbi-cisa-say-ghost-ransomware-actors-compromised-victims-in-more-than-70-countries):
    * Fortinet FortiOS appliances (CVE-2018-13379)
    * Servers running Adobe ColdFusion (CVE-2010-2861 and CVE-2009-3960)
    * Microsoft SharePoint (CVE-2019-0604)
    * Microsoft Exchange (CVE-2021-34473, CVE-2021-34523, and CVE-2021-31207.

scenarioInputs.heading: Defense Level
scenarioInputs.content: * We assume that the SME target has cybersecurity maturity level MIL1 (see [Cybersecurity Capability Maturity Model](https://www.energy.gov/ceser/cybersecurity-capability-maturity-model-c2m2), CCMM) across all maturity domains with MIL2 in some domains. It is assumed that the target generally implements the cybersecurity fundamentals well, and will generally also do a good job of mitigating threats from the most common attack vectors.

scenarioInputs.heading: Intent
scenarioInputs.content:
* The attacker's intent is financial monetization through ransom.
* Specifically we assume that the attacker uses double extortion. This comprises both data encryption ('availability' impact) as well as data exfiltration ('confidentiality' impact). Double extortion is a more complex attack, but can command higher reward than data exfiltration alone. The threat actor demands payment of a ransom in exchange for provision of decryption keys and a promise not to publicise exfiltrated data.
* The greater levels of threat actor skill and capability required to conduct extortion via encryption of systems, over and above the skill required for just data exfiltration extortion, distinguishes this double extortion approach as something that an actor at RAND operational capability level OC3 might be more inclined to do, or be better able to do, than an actor at operational capability level OC2.
* [Coveware](https://www.coveware.com/blog/2023/7/21/ransom-monetization-rates-fall-to-record-low-despite-jump-in-average-ransom-payments?utm_source=chatgpt.com) provides support for the above, indicating how attacks such as double extortion can achieve bigger returns, but require more work on behalf of the threat actor, and the broad skills required may mean that multiple individuals often need to work together in some form of syndicate.

attackSteps.description: The affiliate performs active scanning of internet-facing assets for known vulnerabilities. Some basic OSINT will almost certainly be used to size up victims (e.g. revenue, insurance) to set ransom demands.
attackSteps.failureMode: If recon fails (e.g. no vulnerabilities or intel found), the attack cannot even begin.
attackSteps.breakdownRationale: Active scanning is LLM relevant, must-have. Probability of success is very high (~100%) - so we include the tactic, but do not break it down to technique level for further analysis.

attackSteps.description: The affiliate must obtain or prepare tools, malware, and infrastructure to conduct the attack. In the RaaS model, some resources come ready-made (e.g. the ransomware payload and a leak site for publishing stolen data are provided by the RaaS service).
attackSteps.failureMode: If resource prep fails (e.g. no exploit, no malware or infrastructure), the operation stalls – the affiliate lacks tools or access to proceed.
attackSteps.breakdownRationale: Resource development is essential and at least one aspect could be LLM relevant: Obtaining exploit proofs/code (T1588.005). However, probability of success is very high (~100%) so we stay at tactic level.
techniques: T1583.006 – Acquire Infrastructure: Virtual Private Server

attackSteps.description: In our scenario, the group focuses on vulnerability exploits against Internet-facing systems.
attackSteps.failureMode: If initial access fails (no foothold gained), the affiliate cannot penetrate the victim's network – the ransomware deployment is a non-starter.
attackSteps.breakdownRationale: Must have. Only one technique, so stay at tactic level.

attackSteps.description: After initial access, the affiliate needs to launch tools: e.g. starting a remote shell, running malware. Ransomware operators often use scripts and admin tools for execution.
attackSteps.failureMode: If execution fails (malicious code never runs), the attacker's payloads and commands don't take effect and the attack fails.
attackSteps.breakdownRationale: Must have, but keep at tactic level since though there are alternatives, none seem particularly LLM relevant.

attackSteps.description: Persistence involves maintaining long-term access to systems (surviving reboots, credential changes, etc.). Ransomware affiliates do often implement persistence – for example, adding new accounts – but it's not strictly required if they can complete their attack quickly. Dwell times are reducing and persistence could be optional, hence it is not included.

Ransomware affiliates almost always seek higher privileges after initial entry. Administrative or root access is needed to spread ransomware broadly and to turn off defenses or backups, achieving domain admin on the network is particularly valuable. If this step fails, the adversary's reach is severely limited. With only user-level access, they might not get access to critical servers or data and might be unable to propagate the ransomware across the network.
attackSteps.failureMode: If privilege escalation fails, the attackers are stuck with low-level rights. They may be unable to disable security tools or access sensitive data, often stopping the attack from progressing beyond the initial host.
attackSteps.breakdownRationale: Stay at tactic level, since it's unclear that there is much to be gained by way of LLM estimatability in breaking down to technique level.

attackSteps.description: Defense Evasion is critical when attacking a “well-defended” SME. Ransomware affiliates take active measures to avoid or disable security controls. Although it is essential, it is not included separately since it is implicitly taken into account in other steps, to avoid double-counting.

Credential Access – stealing passwords, hashes, keys, and tickets – is a cornerstone of ransomware operations. However, it is already accounted for under the Privilege Escalation tactic and the associated 'valid accounts' technique. To avoid double counting, and because it is a 'nice to have', the credential access tactic is not included separately.

Once inside, affiliates perform Discovery to understand the environment. This reconnaissance phase inside the network lets them locate where valuable data resides (file servers, databases) and identify defenses or backup systems to circumvent.
attackSteps.failureMode: If internal discovery fails, the attackers may miss critical systems or data. They could encrypt some machines blindly, but might overlook backups or high-value servers, reducing the impact. Lack of knowledge can also lead to mistakes (e.g. tripping alarms) or an incomplete data theft, weakening their leverage.
attackSteps.breakdownRationale: Uncertainty is large regarding how an actor actually proceeds, since there are many potentially applicable techniques. So will stay at the tactic level to avoid overfitting or adding speculative details.

attackSteps.description: Lateral Movement is how the attacker pivots from the initial foothold to broader control of the network. Ransomware affiliates aim to infect as many machines and access as much data as possible. They use techniques like connecting to other systems with stolen credentials or deploying remote execution tools.
attackSteps.failureMode: If lateral movement fails, the compromise remains limited to the initial host or a small subset of systems. The affiliate then might only encrypt a trivial portion of the network or grab a small amount of data, which may not be enough pressure to make the victim pay.
attackSteps.breakdownRationale: Not clear if there is benefit in breaking into technique level, for purposes of determining LLM uplift. So stay at tactic level.

attackSteps.description: In the context of double extortion, Collection of victim data is a must. Before launching ransomware, affiliates quietly search for and aggregate valuable files: databases, client records, intellectual property, emails – anything that would hurt to see leaked publicly.
attackSteps.failureMode: If collection fails (attackers can't gather any sensitive data), the “double” in double-extortion is lost. The attackers would have no files to leak, weakening their bargaining position – they'd be left with just encryption, which many well-prepared victims can recover from.
attackSteps.breakdownRationale: Not clear there is much, if anything, in the way of LLM uplift here, so could stick at tactic level.

attackSteps.description: Command and Control (C2) is the adversary's lifeline into the victim environment. After establishing a foothold, affiliates need a communication channel to send commands, coordinate lateral movement, and extract data. Ransomware operators often repurpose trusted channels: for instance, using standard protocols (HTTP/HTTPS, DNS) or legitimate remote access software to blend in.
attackSteps.failureMode: If C2 fails (the affiliate loses contact with their malware/beacons inside the network), they can't direct the attack. The team would effectively be “locked out” from interacting with compromised systems, stopping further progression like escalation, lateral moves, exfiltration, etc.
attackSteps.breakdownRationale: Not clear there is much scope for using LLMs here, and even if there is, it's not clear that there will be much difference in their potential benefits depending on the specifics of the communication channels used, e.g. whether using HTTPS or hiding communications in DNS traffic etc.

attackSteps.description: Exfiltration is the act of stealing the collected data out to attacker-controlled infrastructure. Double-extortion schemes hinge on this.
attackSteps.failureMode: If exfiltration fails (attackers cannot transmit the stolen data out of the victim's network), the affiliate loses the data-leverage part of the extortion.
attackSteps.breakdownRationale: As for command and control, it's not clear there's much scope for using LLMs here, and even if there is, it's not clear that there will be much difference in their potential benefits depending on the specifics of the exfiltration mechanism. So stay at tactic level.

attackSteps.description: The Impact phase is where the attackers encrypt data on all reachable systems. This renders the victim organization's files and services unusable, causing operational paralysis. Affiliates also take additional impact actions: they often inhibit system recovery by deleting backups and shadow volume copies. It is common that ransomware even changes wallpapers or drops ransom notes in directories to ensure the victim knows they've been hit.
attackSteps.failureMode: If impact fails (e.g. files don't get encrypted or critical services remain unaffected), the victim's operations continue relatively unscathed. With no significant disruption or data loss, the victim has little incentive to pay, and the affiliate's attack effectively fails to achieve its goal.
attackSteps.breakdownRationale: It's not clear there's much scope for using LLMs for the encryption and data destruction related techniques (T1486, T1490, T1489, T1485). However, LLMs might be used to assist with the social engineering (T1657) related to the extortion negotiations. Hence we separate out this technique.

nodes.name: Reconnaissance
nodes.baselineRationale: Adversaries commonly discover exposed systems and known vulnerabilities via [active scanning](https://attack.mitre.org/tactics/TA0043/), which is almost guaranteed to succeed if any services are exposed. The affiliate performs active scanning of internet-facing assets for known vulnerabilities, and some basic OSINT will almost certainly be used to size up victims to set ransom demands.
nodes.sotaRationale: Not elicited due to high baseline probability.
nodes.saturatedRationale: Not elicited due to high baseline probability.

nodes.name: Resource Development
nodes.baselineRationale: Ransomware affiliates will almost certainly gather the needed exploits and malware before attacking. This includes purchasing exploit code for known CVEs and [RaaS](https://www.ncsc.gov.uk/whitepaper/ransomware-extortion-and-the-cyber-crime-ecosystem) payloads, renting servers, and so on. Defenders cannot directly interfere at this stage. Success is effectively assured.
nodes.sotaRationale: Not elicited due to high baseline probability.
nodes.saturatedRationale: Not elicited due to high baseline probability.

nodes.name: Initial Access
nodes.baselineRationale: These probabilities are conditioned on the active scanning of the Reconnaissance tactic having identified that the particular SME has a vulnerable public-facing web application. While SMEs might have firewalls or WAFs, these will not necessarily cover all attacks and may be misconfigured. An SME may not rigorously isolate DMZ systems or adequately enforce least privilege on service accounts. [Qualys (2023)](https://blog.qualys.com/vulnerabilities-threat-research/2023/12/19/2023-threat-landscape-year-in-review-part-one) reports that over 7,000 vulnerabilities had proof-of-concept exploit code, and 206 had weaponised exploit code that is highly likely to compromise the target system. Attackers will naturally select vulnerabilities and associated exploits with high probabilities of success and good options for onward escalation. The wide credible interval reflects significant uncertainty — probability of success depends heavily on the specific CVE and exploit used.
nodes.sotaRationale: An LLM at this capability level can reliably generate and adapt exploits for known n-day vulnerabilities, providing OC3 actors with more reliable exploit code and faster adaptation that reduces failures from coding errors. The LLM excels at exploit adaptation, troubleshooting, and payload modification — directly addressing key technical bottlenecks. However, the LLM does not address critical remaining obstacles: identifying which specific vulnerabilities targets actually have, bypassing configured defences, or adapting to novel target environments. Against MIL1/MIL2 SMEs with some patching but possible misconfiguration, the LLM serves as a force multiplier for well-documented exploitation tasks — rapidly synthesising public exploits, generating supporting scripts, and troubleshooting common errors — but its limitations become critical at the boundary of its capabilities, where it may introduce errors and struggle with environment-specific adaptations.
nodes.saturatedRationale: An LLM at this capability ceiling is significantly overqualified for typical n-day exploits against public-facing SME applications. Common initial access vectors such as VPN CVEs, SharePoint vulnerabilities, and web application exploits are much simpler than the benchmark, with abundant public proof-of-concept code available. The LLM provides meaningful assistance in key bottlenecks including exploit customisation, defence bypass adaptation, and troubleshooting failed attempts. It can optimise exploit selection, provide reliable customisation, and facilitate effective debugging — all of which are bottlenecks for resource-constrained OC3 groups. However, real-world operational friction including network conditions, environmental variability, and timing continues to limit impact. Some failure modes such as patched systems, sophisticated defences, and environmental factors remain unaffected by LLM assistance regardless of its capability level.

nodes.name: Execution
nodes.baselineRationale: Execution — adversary-controlled code running on victim systems — will have to be successfully performed multiple times on multiple machines as the attacker moves laterally and seeks to encrypt data on different systems. Ransomware affiliates commonly use PowerShell, script interpreters, or built-in tools for execution. SMEs rarely have strict application whitelisting or behavioural anti-malware controls. Skilled attackers can evade AV through malware code obfuscation or memory-resident techniques. [Barracuda XDR](https://cybersecurityasia.net/ransomware-stopped-during-lateral-movement/) reports that 6% of security alerts in Q1 2025 were for suspicious PowerShell scripts, while [Expert Insights](https://expertinsights.com/endpoint-security/edr-market-overview) (2024) suggests roughly 50% of companies had XDR. If the attacker moves quickly and the SME is poorly equipped in terms of SOC staff, EDR may not catch the attack in time. The wide credible interval reflects substantial uncertainty and the lack of good source material.
nodes.sotaRationale: The LLM provides modest incremental value by accelerating command generation and suggesting documented living-off-the-land techniques. However, the OC3 actors are already technically skilled, and the execution phase relies more on operational tradecraft than code analysis capabilities. The LLM can assist with basic scripting and simple obfuscation patterns but lacks the sophistication for advanced behavioural evasion of EDR. Against MIL1/MIL2 SME targets with limited detection capabilities, the LLM can help generate payload variants and troubleshoot execution failures, but the primary bottleneck — sophisticated evasion of active defences — remains largely unaddressed. Some experts noted that the LLM's weak correlation between code analysis and real-time operational tradecraft limits its contribution, while others emphasised that even modest acceleration in script generation and obfuscation could compound meaningfully across multiple execution attempts.
nodes.saturatedRationale: An LLM at this capability level demonstrates expert-level exploitation and evasion capabilities that translate moderately well to the execution phase. The primary value lies in sophisticated obfuscation generation that reduces AV and EDR detection rates, broader technique variety, and faster iteration cycles. While there is a domain gap between Python web exploitation and Windows or Linux command execution, the fundamental skills — obfuscation, defence bypass, system internals understanding — are applicable. Against SME targets with MIL1/MIL2 maturity and no 24/7 SOC, the LLM provides meaningful enhancement through superior obfuscation, reduced operator errors, and rapid technique adaptation. However, execution failures from environmental factors, network monitoring, and application controls remain largely unaffected. The improvement is ultimately constrained by the fact that OC3 actors already possess strong execution capabilities and the target environment's weak defences mean execution was already relatively successful at baseline.

nodes.name: Privilege Escalation
nodes.baselineRationale: Ransomware attackers almost always try to gain admin-level control. Relevant techniques include [T1068 – Exploitation for Privilege Escalation](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1068/) using exploits like ZeroLogon or PrintNightmare, OR [T1003 – OS Credential Dumping](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1003/) to access password hashes from memory. SMEs may have flat, non-segmented networks with many local admins. Attackers may dump local credentials and find an admin password that works across systems. Low [MFA adoption](https://jumpcloud.com/blog/multi-factor-authentication-statistics) (34% at medium-sized companies versus 87% at large enterprises) and informal admin account management make it easier to leverage stolen accounts. There is a fairly high chance of success in privilege escalation, particularly given low MFA usage and relatively flat, small network structures where one compromised admin credential can give system-wide access. The credible interval bounds are large due to the lack of good statistical information.
nodes.sotaRationale: The LLM at this capability level can meaningfully assist with privilege escalation by providing systematic enumeration guidance, correct tool syntax, and multiple technique suggestions. It demonstrates competence with documented vulnerability patterns and standard exploitation techniques — functioning at roughly a junior security analyst level — which is sufficient for most SME privilege escalation scenarios. The LLM's primary value is efficiency: optimising technique selection, providing correct command syntax for tools like Mimikatz, and reducing troubleshooting time. However, the OC3 actors already have a relatively high baseline success rate, limiting uplift potential. The LLM assists with a substantial portion of baseline failure cases through better enumeration and troubleshooting, but its ceiling-level capabilities mean it struggles with target-specific configurations beyond its training data. Environmental factors such as patching status, EDR presence, and network segmentation continue to drive the remaining failures.
nodes.saturatedRationale: An LLM at this capability ceiling — solving expert-level deserialization chains and complex exploitation tasks — vastly exceeds the capability needed for SME privilege escalation, which primarily involves applying well-known techniques against limited defences. The LLM provides systematic enumeration guidance, optimal tool selection, effective troubleshooting, and alternative vector suggestions, addressing key bottlenecks in the baseline success rate. Experts noted meaningful improvement through accelerated technique selection, exploit adaptation, filling skill gaps in unfamiliar scenarios, and perfect command execution that reduces operator errors. However, improvement is constrained by the already-high baseline indicating OC3's existing competence, and environmental factors — patching, EDR, and network segmentation — continue to drive a significant portion of failures that no amount of technical assistance can overcome.

nodes.name: Discovery
nodes.baselineRationale: Affiliates need to map the network — find servers, shares, and backups. In an SME, networks are sometimes flat and lacking segmentation, and in such cases the attacker's malware may freely scan or query Active Directory. Detection of internal reconnaissance is generally less likely, especially if the attacker uses stealthy techniques such as legitimate admin commands or slow scanning. [Barracuda XDR](https://cybersecurityasia.net/ransomware-stopped-during-lateral-movement/) reports that 48% of security alerts in H1 2024 were for network reconnaissance, and [Expert Insights](https://expertinsights.com/endpoint-security/edr-market-overview) (2024) suggests roughly 50% of companies had XDR. There is a high likelihood that the attacker can perform necessary discovery operations, particularly if privilege escalation has already been achieved.
nodes.sotaRationale: Not elicited due to high baseline probability.
nodes.saturatedRationale: Not elicited due to high baseline probability.

nodes.name: Lateral Movement
nodes.baselineRationale: In a small or medium business, once the attacker has credentials or admin access, moving laterally is straightforward. All machines often trust the domain admin or common credentials. Many SMEs do not restrict admin share access. Without network segmentation, the threat actor can reach most systems over the network. [Barracuda XDR](https://cybersecurityasia.net/ransomware-stopped-during-lateral-movement/) indicates that just under half of ransomware attacks in H1 2024 were spotted by the lateral movement detection engine. SMEs are less likely to implement robust EDR/XDR and associated well-staffed SOC resources. If the SME has an isolated backup server, that may prevent lateral movement to all necessary machines. [Kent Invicta (citing Beaming research, 2024)](https://www.kentinvictachamber.co.uk/members-blog/half-of-uk-smes-have-lost-vital-data-in-the-last-five-years/) reports that only one in four UK businesses consistently pursue good backup practices, and 34% maintain an air-gapped data backup physically isolated from the internet.
nodes.sotaRationale: The LLM provides a modest uplift through more systematic target selection and execution efficiency. The improvement is constrained by the already-high baseline capability of OC3 actors, the LLM's intermediate capability ceiling limiting sophisticated evasion, and structural barriers such as network segmentation and credential scope limitations that better execution alone cannot overcome. Experts broadly agreed that lateral movement with admin credentials on flat networks is foundational ransomware tradecraft, and the LLM operates comfortably below its capability ceiling on this task. Its primary value lies in reducing technical execution errors through better automation, supporting less experienced team members, and providing systematic enumeration — but the dominant failure mode of network segmentation is an environmental constraint that technical assistance cannot address.
nodes.saturatedRationale: An LLM at this capability ceiling is vastly overqualified for lateral movement with admin credentials, which involves standard techniques like RDP and SMB. The LLM can meaningfully reduce technical execution errors through better automation and scripting, improve enumeration and path optimisation, and provide more efficient troubleshooting. Experts estimated that it addresses a substantial proportion of skill-based failures through perfect command syntax, automated scripting, and strategic optimisation. However, the primary bottleneck causing baseline failures is architectural — network isolation, air-gapped backups, and credential scope limitations — which no amount of AI assistance can overcome. The OC3 actor's baseline already indicates strong technical capability, so the marginal value of even expert-level assistance is inherently limited for this operationally straightforward task.

nodes.name: Collection
nodes.baselineRationale: To maximise extortion, the affiliate will quietly collect confidential files before encryption. In SMEs, if the attacker has achieved admin privileges, the attacker can read most or all data. SMEs generally lack DLP or alerts for large file access. If the attacker creates archives of data or stages data on an internal server, it is unlikely to be detected. There are essentially no mitigations to prevent data gathering — it abuses normal file access channels. According to [StrongDM](https://www.strongdm.com/blog/small-business-cyber-security-statistics), only 17% of small businesses encrypt data. In most cases the attacker will be able to collect data, though there is some chance that some enterprises may have encrypted data that the attacker cannot access.
nodes.sotaRationale: Not elicited due to high baseline probability.
nodes.saturatedRationale: Not elicited due to high baseline probability.

nodes.name: Command and Control
nodes.baselineRationale: After initial compromise, the affiliate's malware needs to communicate outbound to receive instructions. Outbound web traffic is usually unrestricted aside from perhaps basic web filtering. Attackers commonly use HTTPS, web protocols, or even legitimate cloud services for C2, which are unlikely to be blocked. Without dedicated egress filtering, intrusion prevention, or pre-configured detections, SMEs will generally not notice the C2 traffic. The adversary might also use encryption (TLS) or proxy through benign hosts, and SMEs lack the means to detect this subtle traffic.
nodes.sotaRationale: Not elicited due to high baseline probability.
nodes.saturatedRationale: Not elicited due to high baseline probability.

nodes.name: Exfiltration
nodes.baselineRationale: The affiliate will attempt to send stolen data to their own servers before launching ransomware. If the SME does not have DLP (and most are assumed not to), then there may be little to prevent exfiltration, though it may be noticed by large usage or bills for data traffic out of the network, or more basic threshold-type rules monitoring the network. Attackers might upload data via HTTPS to cloud storage or use their C2 channels. SMEs typically will not detect an HTTPS upload to an unknown server. The exfiltration might be limited by bandwidth or noticed if it crashes a network link.
nodes.sotaRationale: Not elicited due to high baseline probability.
nodes.saturatedRationale: Not elicited due to high baseline probability.

nodes.name: T1486/T1490/T1489 - Data Encryption and System Disruption
nodes.baselineRationale: In the final stage, the affiliate deploys the ransomware payload to encrypt data and disrupt operations, and they try to destroy backups to prevent recovery. Whilst most SMEs do maintain backups, often these are online or accessible from the network. According to [Invenio IT](https://invenioit.com/continuity/disaster-recovery-statistics/), 58% of backups fail during recovery due to factors like outdated technology, inadequate testing, or malware infection — hence even if the threat actor did not reach the backup servers, there is a good chance that the backups will prove insufficient. [Sophos](https://assets.sophos.com/X24WTUEQ/at/9brgj5n44hqvgsp5f5bqcps/sophos-state-of-ransomware-2024-wp.pdf) reports that 57% of backup compromise attempts were successful. Some endpoint security might detect mass encryption, but SMEs will not always have enabled or configured these. By the time the attacker has reached this stage, they will have established significant privileges and control, and there is a good chance this last impact step will succeed.
nodes.sotaRationale: As a RaaS affiliate, the OC3 group already has deep operational expertise in ransomware deployment — this is their core function. The LLM's capability ceiling demonstrates procedural execution ability but in a different technical domain. At this attack stage with admin privileges and pre-built tools, success depends primarily on target backup architecture rather than attacker knowledge gaps. The LLM provides marginal value through systematic procedural guidance including more thorough backup enumeration, complete coverage checking, and deployment troubleshooting. However, the high baseline and the fact that most failures stem from organisational defences — particularly offline or immutable backups — limit the uplift. The remaining failure cases are primarily structural rather than tactical gaps that an LLM can address, and the actor's existing RaaS tools already handle the core deployment effectively.
nodes.saturatedRationale: An LLM with expert-level capabilities far exceeds the technical requirements for ransomware deployment scripting, but provides limited marginal value because OC3 already uses sophisticated RaaS platforms and the main failure mode is target defences rather than attacker technical capability. The LLM can write more comprehensive backup enumeration scripts, improve execution robustness across diverse environments, and better identify recovery mechanisms. Experts noted that it addresses a portion of technical and implementation errors through real-time troubleshooting, comprehensive automation, and exhaustive knowledge of backup destruction techniques. However, architectural defences such as offline and immutable backups constitute a meaningful share of the target population and cannot be overcome regardless of attacker capability. The modest improvement reflects the task being far below the LLM's capability ceiling while already having high baseline success with professional tools.

nodes.name: T1657 - Extortion
nodes.baselineRationale: [Chainanalysis](https://www.chainalysis.com/blog/crypto-crime-ransomware-victim-extortion-2025/) reports that around 30% of negotiations actually lead to payments, a figure also supported by [Coveware](https://www.coveware.com/blog/2025/4/29/the-organizational-structure-of-ransomware-threat-actor-groups-is-evolving-before-our-eyes). There appears to be a trend in the literature indicating more reluctance to pay ransoms. The lower bound reflects reports of companies becoming more reluctant to pay, while the upper bound acknowledges that some sources report higher payment rates.
nodes.sotaRationale: The LLM's demonstrated technical capability translates weakly to extortion success due to a fundamental domain mismatch — extortion requires sophisticated human psychology, adaptive negotiation, and contextual business understanding rather than technical exploitation skills. While the LLM can meaningfully improve ransom note professionalism, payment instruction clarity, and basic negotiation scripting, the majority of extortion success factors — victim financial capacity, backup quality, policy constraints, and risk tolerance — remain outside attacker control regardless of LLM assistance. Experts broadly agreed that the OC3 actors, as RaaS affiliates, already employ proven communication templates and negotiation tactics. The LLM provides marginal optimisation rather than transformation, helping with better victim research and more professional communications but not addressing the fundamental determinants of whether a victim decides to pay.
nodes.saturatedRationale: The LLM's expert-level capabilities are in a fundamentally different domain than extortion, which requires social, psychological, and business judgement skills. The primary determinants of extortion success — victim financial capability, backup and recovery posture, organisational policy, and regulatory environment — are unchanged by LLM assistance. OC3 already operates professionally with $1M in resources, limiting marginal gains from communication improvements. Experts noted that while the LLM could provide modest improvements in victim research, multilingual communication, and ransom note professionalism, these enhancements address only secondary bottlenecks. The established RaaS affiliate processes and playbooks already cover the core negotiation workflow, meaning even a maximally capable LLM provides only incremental benefit to this fundamentally social and economic step.

nodes.name: Number of Actors
nodes.baselineRationale: An 'actor' here refers to the cybercrime syndicate as a whole (approximately ten individuals). Working from an estimated pool of roughly [700 affiliates](https://www.kindus.co.uk/lockbit-ransomware-takedown/) across the major RaaS operations, approximately one-third are assumed to be OC3-level groups. Of these, around 40% target SMEs in the $10M–$50M revenue bracket, with roughly [40% using double extortion](https://firewalltimes.com/ransomware-statistics/). Applying the [Sophos](https://www.sophos.com/en-us/content/state-of-ransomware) figure that approximately 29% of initial accesses use vulnerability exploits yields approximately ten affiliates matching this scenario profile. The lower bound accounts for the possibility that a much smaller fraction of affiliates are OC3-level and target this segment, while the upper bound reflects scenarios where a larger share of affiliates operate at this capability level and target SMEs.
nodes.sotaRationale: An LLM at this capability level provides tactical assistance primarily for the initial access phase, representing a modest fraction of total operational complexity for OC3 ransomware operations. The baseline of ten actors already represents groups with substantial organisational capacity. These groups already possess basic technical capabilities through existing tools such as Metasploit and ExploitDB. The LLM primarily accelerates reconnaissance and exploit adaptation but does not address post-exploitation complexity, evasion capabilities, or the organisational and resource barriers that are the main constraints on actor population. Experts noted that the LLM cannot solve the human coordination problem, and most OC3-level actors already possess equivalent or superior capability to the benchmark level. The modest increase reflects marginal efficiency gains and perhaps a small number of actors at the capability boundary being enabled.
nodes.saturatedRationale: An LLM at this capability level significantly reduces the technical exploitation barrier, which is a major bottleneck for this attack scenario. However, the scenario still requires OC3-level organisational capacity — approximately ten people, around $1M in resources — along with RaaS relationships and operational tradecraft that the LLM cannot provide. Experts estimated meaningful but bounded growth in the actor population, with the primary impact being on groups at the OC2/OC3 capability boundary who can now more reliably exploit public vulnerabilities, and on existing OC3 groups who gain efficiency. The technical barrier reduction is partially offset by persistent non-technical constraints including organisational capacity, criminal infrastructure access, and law enforcement risk that remain unchanged regardless of LLM capability.

nodes.name: Number of Attempts per Actor
nodes.baselineRationale: The number of attacks refers to the count of times an exploit is attempted against a particular web-facing application or device, not the number scanned during reconnaissance. Three estimates were triangulated: 150 from operational capacity analysis (based on approximately 5 person-days per attack attempt with a team of 10), 350 derived from historical numbers on successes per individual with an assumed probability of successful attack, and 400 based on team-motivational factors. More emphasis was placed on the operational capacity figures, yielding a best estimate of 200 attacks per year. [Secureworks/Sophos](https://www.secureworks.com/about/press/ransomware-dwell-time-hits-low-of-24-hours) report that median dwell time fell from 4.5 days in 2022 to less than 24 hours in 2023, driven by criminals' desire for lower detection risk. There is significant uncertainty due to the lack of useful historical information on failed attack attempts.
nodes.sotaRationale: The LLM provides moderate efficiency improvements to OC3 ransomware operations through faster exploit adaptation, automated scripting for routine tasks, and reduced time in tool customisation. These gains are partially offset by LLM error debugging overhead and constrained by unchanging operational realities: target availability, infrastructure requirements, OPSEC concerns, and law enforcement risk. The LLM's capability ceiling — basic pattern matching for known vulnerabilities — provides modest assistance primarily in reconnaissance and exploit adaptation phases, but the primary bottleneck for OC3 actors — sophisticated post-exploitation tradecraft while evading detection — remains essentially unchanged. Experts estimated efficiency gains of roughly 10–20% in the technical phases, which translate to a smaller overall increase because technical phases represent only part of the total attack workflow.
nodes.saturatedRationale: An LLM at this capability level has capabilities far exceeding what OC3 needs for n-day exploitation, providing significant operational efficiency gains by automating scripting, reducing post-exploitation time, and accelerating tool customisation. Experts estimated roughly 20–35% efficiency gains in technical phases, with the primary impact being dramatic acceleration of the exploitation bottleneck and significant post-exploitation efficiency improvements. However, operational tempo is constrained by infrastructure-bound tasks such as reconnaissance and OPSEC, detection risk management, and resource limits that the LLM does not address. Human bottlenecks in decision-making and quality control, uncertainty about capability transfer from Python to diverse SME technologies, and error correction overhead from LLM hallucinations further limit the overall throughput increase.

nodes.name: Ransom Payment
nodes.baselineRationale: Based on [Sophos (2025)](https://www.sophos.com/en-us/content/state-of-ransomware) data for SMEs in the $10M–$50M revenue bracket, the median payout is $106k. Using the mean-to-median ratio from the [Sophos (2024)](https://assets.sophos.com/X24WTUEQ/at/9brgj5n44hqvgsp5f5bqcps/sophos-state-of-ransomware-2024-wp.pdf) report yields an estimated mean payout of approximately $550k. This is weighted by the probability of ransom payout given a technically successful attack: [Chainanalysis](https://www.chainalysis.com/blog/crypto-crime-ransomware-victim-extortion-2025/) and [Coveware](https://www.coveware.com/blog/2025/4/29/the-organizational-structure-of-ransomware-threat-actor-groups-is-evolving-before-our-eyes) both indicate approximately 30% of negotiations lead to payments. The resulting best estimate for expected ransom payout per technically successful attack is approximately $165k. The lower bound reflects uncertainty in actual mean payouts and possible deflation, while the upper bound assumes a higher payment rate. There is a great deal of volatility in payments year on year, with [Sophos](https://www.sophos.com/en-us/press/press-releases/2024/04/ransomware-payments-increase-500-last-year-finds-sophos-state) reporting up to 500% swings.
nodes.sotaRationale: The LLM capability at this level provides marginal operational efficiency in reconnaissance and initial access phases but offers virtually no enhancement to the critical phases — lateral movement, privilege escalation, and strategic targeting — that determine ransomware payment amounts. OC3-level actors already possess the technical capabilities this LLM provides. Experts generally agreed that any uplift in mean ransom payment is modest, primarily from minor improvements in data collection thoroughness and exfiltration completeness that enhance double-extortion leverage. Some experts noted that improved data targeting could yield slightly better outcomes, while others emphasised that baseline uncertainty in ransom payments far exceeds any LLM contribution. The wide uncertainty range in estimates primarily reflects baseline distribution uncertainty rather than LLM-related variability.
nodes.saturatedRationale: An LLM with expert-level technical capabilities provides meaningful but bounded uplift in mean ransom payments through improved data exfiltration completeness, better operational security enabling more thorough attacks, and enhanced technical execution reliability. Experts estimated improvements driven by better identification of sensitive data, more accurate demand calibration, and more complete system compromise that increases recovery difficulty. However, the benefit is bounded by hard SME payment capacity constraints, the LLM operating at its capability ceiling which introduces errors requiring human intervention, and the baseline competence of OC3-level actors. The primary determinants of ransom payment — victim financial capacity, backup and recovery posture, and willingness to pay — remain largely unaffected by the LLM's technical capabilities.

nodes.name: Recovery Cost
nodes.baselineRationale: Recovery costs for any specific attack vary according to a long list of factors including whether the attacker completes the full double-extortion attack, whether the ransom is paid, whether backups exist and are intact, and the value of exfiltrated data. [Sophos (2025)](https://www.sophos.com/en-us/content/state-of-ransomware) reports that for companies of 100–250 employees, recovery costs were approximately $638k, and as averaged across all company sizes, recovery cost decreased from $2.83M in 2024 to $1.53M in 2025. Sophos' survey question covered downtime, people time, device cost, network cost, and lost opportunity. The best estimate of approximately $650k is based on the Sophos (2025) figure for the relevant company size bracket. The lower bound allows for possible deflation in ransomware costs, while the upper bound reflects the significantly higher figures seen in 2024.
nodes.sotaRationale: The LLM's intermediate capabilities provide marginal benefit to OC3 actors, primarily through increased consistency and systematisation rather than novel techniques. Against MIL1/MIL2 SMEs, the net impact is a modest increase over baseline through more systematic execution and faster progression through standard attack patterns, particularly in discovery, collection, and impact phases. The primary uplift comes from more complete system encryption and data exfiltration that increases restoration and breach response costs, but real-world friction from LLM errors partially offsets gains. Experts noted that the OC3 group already demonstrates capabilities at or above the benchmark level, so the LLM provides marginal improvements in execution consistency and enumeration thoroughness. The wide range in estimates reflects both baseline uncertainty about ransomware impact variability and modest additional uncertainty about LLM effectiveness.
nodes.saturatedRationale: An LLM with elite technical capabilities increases mean recovery costs through superior automation of attack execution — more complete system encryption, thorough data exfiltration, faster execution, and better persistence. The primary impact mechanisms are extended dwell time through better evasion, more thorough encryption and backup destruction, and more complete data exfiltration. Experts estimated meaningful improvements in data discovery, lateral movement completeness, and operational security, though there is significant domain mismatch between cryptanalysis expertise and operational ransomware execution. The improvement is constrained by finite SME target size and the professional baseline of an OC3 group already using RaaS tools. The wide uncertainty range captures high variability in how automation advantages translate to real-world recovery costs.
